title: aggregate-spatial-data
text: Besides building grids, package includes functions aggregating spatial data. start, let’s load packages: Now let’s get raster data throw grid : Note use functions get_center, get_min, get_max get extent coordinates objects (sf terra). Now plot, just make sure grid makes sense:  Now can use grid aggregate data covers: Just note column names built results: Besides identifier cell grid (grid_id), columns combination aggregation function (funs), input raster (meuse), category (didn’t include one example). names column results build order, separated points. exception n, correspond number raster cells included cell aggregation grid. Now, can, example, plot total value cell aggregation grid:  mean value:
code: library(sf) #> Linking to GEOS 3.12.1, GDAL 3.8.4, PROJ 9.4.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE library(terra) #> terra 1.8.93 library(treeslabgrid) r <- rast(system.file("ex/meuse.tif", package = "terra")) g <- make_grid_origin_res(   xy_origin = get_center(r),   xy_min = get_min(r),   xy_max = get_max(r),   cell_size = 500,   crs = terra::crs(r),   id_col = "grid_id" ) plot(r, reset = FALSE) plot(st_cast(g[get_geom_colname(g)], "LINESTRING"), add = TRUE) meuse_agg <- aggregate_raster(   x = r,   by = NA_character_,   grid = g,   grid_id = "grid_id",   funs = c("sum", "min", "mean", "max"),   na.rm = TRUE ) colnames(meuse_agg) #>  [1] "grid_id"      "sum.n.1"      "min.n.1"      "mean.n.1"     "max.n.1"      #>  [6] "max.meuse.1"  "mean.meuse.1" "min.meuse.1"  "sum.meuse.1"  "x" plot(meuse_agg["sum.meuse.1"]) plot(meuse_agg["mean.meuse.1"])

title: grids
text: package includes functions building regular vector grids can used aggregate spatial data. achieve , package includes extends function st_make_grid provided package sf. load package, just type:

what: Make a grid using geographic data as a template
title: grids
text: Imagine like cover raster 5x5 grid. can achieve , first load data: call make_grid funcion: Finally, plot results ensure right:  call st_cast converts grid’s cells polygons lines, allowing us see plot. Note make_grid also works vector data:  Also note warning shape cells; since specify cell size, resulting grid built using rectangles instead squares.
code: library(sf) #> Linking to GEOS 3.12.1, GDAL 3.8.4, PROJ 9.4.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE library(terra) #> terra 1.8.93 filename <- system.file("ex/meuse.tif", package = "terra") r <- rast(filename) g <- make_grid(r, n = c(5, 5), cellsize = 1e3) plot(r, reset = FALSE) plot(st_cast(g["geometry"], "LINESTRING"), add = TRUE) fname <- system.file("shape/nc.shp", package = "sf") v <- st_read(fname, quiet = TRUE)  g <- make_grid(v, n = c(5, 5)) #> Warning in make_grid(v, n = c(5, 5)): The grid's cells aren't squares!  plot(st_cast(v["geometry"], "MULTILINESTRING"), col = "gray", reset = FALSE) plot(g["geometry"], add = TRUE)

title: grids
text: Alternatively, don’t geographic data use template, can create grids using numeric parameters. following examples show .

what: Build grids using coordinates and the number of cells
title: grids
text: Imagine , reason, need constrain number cells grid. case, minimum maximum coordinates, can use make_grid_min_max_cells function. example, let us assume minimum maximum data showed limited 35 cells grid. Using data, can build grid: add Coodinate Reference System since default, funcion assumes longitude latitude want grid meters.
code: min_coords <- ext(r)[c(1, 3)] max_coords <- ext(r)[c(2, 4)] center_coords <- min_coords + ((max_coords - min_coords) / 2) number_of_cells <- c(7, 5) g <- make_grid_min_max_cells(   xy_min = min_coords,   xy_max = max_coords,   n = number_of_cells,   crs = 9001 ) #> Warning in make_grid(x, n = n, id_col = id_col, add_row_col = add_row_col, : #> The grid's cells aren't squares! plot(r, reset = FALSE) plot(st_cast(g["geometry"], "LINESTRING"), add = TRUE)

what: Build grids using origin, distance, and number of cells
title: grids
text: ocassions, know point want grid’s cells meet; let us call point origin. example, can use top-right corner data origin point, , maximum coordinates computed . use origin point grid: Now grid toches maximum coordinates data space remaining bottom-left cell.
code: g <- make_grid_origin_dist(   xy_origin = max_coords,   n = number_of_cells,   cell_size = 500,   crs = 9001 ) plot(r, reset = FALSE) plot(st_cast(g["geometry"], "LINESTRING"), add = TRUE)

what: Build grids using origin and resolution
title: grids
text: can use point origin grid , given cell size, can build grid goes minimum maximum coordinates.
code: g <- make_grid_origin_res(   xy_origin = center_coords,   xy_min = min_coords,   xy_max = max_coords,   cell_size = 500 )

title: grids
text: polygon grid identified integer.  possible add identifiers row column:
code: g <- make_grid(v,   n = c(9, 3),   cellsize = c(1, 1),   add_centroids = TRUE )  plot(st_cast(g["geometry"], "LINESTRING"), reset = FALSE) text(x = g[["X"]], y = g[["Y"]], labels = g[["id"]]) g <- make_grid(v,   n = c(9, 3),   cellsize = c(1, 1),   add_row_col = TRUE,   add_centroids = TRUE ) g["gid"] <- paste(g[["grid_row"]], g[["grid_col"]], sep = ",")  plot(st_cast(g["geometry"], "LINESTRING"), reset = FALSE) text(x = g[["X"]], y = g[["Y"]], labels = g[["gid"]])

what: Create Grids and Use Them to Aggregate Data
title: Create Grids and Use Them to Aggregate Data
text: goal treeslabgrid help users aggregate spatial data using regular grids. achieve , treeslabgrid extends sf terra packages, providing additional functions create grids aggregate data using many aggregation functions simultaneously.

title: Create Grids and Use Them to Aggregate Data
text: can install development version treeslabgrid GitHub :

title: Create Grids and Use Them to Aggregate Data
text: basic example shows create grid use aggregate raster. First, let’s load packages: Now, let’s get raster examples terra package: treeslabgrid offers many ways create grid covers spatial data. example, can center grid middle raster’s extent: can plot just : grid seems OK; , can now aggregate raster data using : Note use many aggregation functions time, , results present results one:
code: library(treeslabgrid) library(sf) #> Linking to GEOS 3.13.1, GDAL 3.10.3, PROJ 9.6.0; sf_use_s2() is TRUE library(terra) #> terra 1.8.93 # Get a raster. r <- rast(system.file("ex/meuse.tif", package = "terra")) # Create a grid over that covers the raster. g <- make_grid_origin_res(   xy_origin = get_center(r),   xy_min = get_min(r),   xy_max = get_max(r),   cell_size = 500,   crs = terra::crs(r),   id_col = "grid_id" ) plot(r, reset = FALSE) plot(st_cast(g[get_geom_colname(g)], "LINESTRING"), add = TRUE) meuse_agg <- aggregate_raster(   x = r,   by = NA_character_,   grid = g,   grid_id = "grid_id",   funs = c("sum", "min", "mean", "max"),   na.rm = TRUE ) plot(meuse_agg["sum.meuse.1"]) plot(meuse_agg["min.meuse.1"]) plot(meuse_agg["mean.meuse.1"]) plot(meuse_agg["max.meuse.1"])

what: Build a grid that can be used later for aggregating geographic data — make_grid
title: Build a grid that can be used later for aggregating geographic data — make_grid
text: Build grid around spatial data (vector raster) scratch. make_grid returns grid around spatial data (vector raster). make_grid_min_max_cells returns grid using minimum & maximum coordinates, number cells grid. make_grid_origin_dist returns grid using coordinates origin point (minimum XY), number cells, number cells grid. make_grid_origin_res returns grid using coordinates origin point inside area cell size. new grid vertex origin fit many cells possible area.

title: Build a grid that can be used later for aggregating geographic data — make_grid
code: make_grid(   x,   n,   cellsize = c(diff(sf::st_bbox(x)[c(1, 3)]), diff(sf::st_bbox(x)[c(2, 4)]))/n,   id_col = "id",   add_row_col = FALSE,   add_centroids = FALSE,   add_area = FALSE,   drop_area_units = FALSE )  make_grid_min_max_cells(   xy_min,   xy_max,   n,   crs = 4326,   id_col = "id",   add_row_col = FALSE,   add_centroids = FALSE,   add_area = FALSE,   drop_area_units = FALSE )  make_grid_origin_dist(   xy_origin,   n,   cell_size,   crs,   id_col = "id",   add_row_col = FALSE,   add_centroids = FALSE,   add_area = FALSE,   drop_area_units = FALSE )  make_grid_origin_res(   xy_origin,   xy_min,   xy_max,   cell_size,   crs = 4326,   id_col = "id" )

title: Build a grid that can be used later for aggregating geographic data — make_grid
text: x sf terra object. n integer. Number cells (columns, rows). cellsize numeric length 1 2 (width, length). defaults width heigth bounding box given object divided number cells. id_col character length 1. Name column identifying grid cells. add_row_col Either logical(1) character(2). columns identifying row column cell added? , provide names; otherwise default names used add_centroids Either logical(1) character(2). columns coordinates cells' centroids added? , provide names (X & Y); otherwise default names used. add_area Either logical(1) character(1). column cell's area added? , provide name; otherwise default name used. drop_area_units logical. Drop area units? xy_min, xy_max pair numeric vectors minimum maximum coordiantes grid. crs Numeric character representing Coordinate Reference System. xy_origin numeric(2) representing XY point. cell_size numeric length 1 2. width heigth o cell. units crs.

title: Build a grid that can be used later for aggregating geographic data — make_grid
text: sf object polygons.

title: Build a grid that can be used later for aggregating geographic data — make_grid
code: # Make a 5x5 grid covering a polygon. pol <- sf::st_sfc(   sf::st_polygon(list(cbind(     c(0, 3, 3, 0, 0),     c(0, 0, 3, 3, 0)   ))),   crs = 4326 ) make_grid(pol, cellsize = 1, n = 5) #> Simple feature collection with 25 features and 1 field #> Geometry type: POLYGON #> Dimension:     XY #> Bounding box:  xmin: 0 ymin: 0 xmax: 5 ymax: 5 #> Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84 #> First 10 features: #>    id                       geometry #> 1   1 POLYGON ((0 0, 1 0, 1 1, 0 ... #> 2   2 POLYGON ((1 0, 2 0, 2 1, 1 ... #> 3   3 POLYGON ((2 0, 3 0, 3 1, 2 ... #> 4   4 POLYGON ((3 0, 4 0, 4 1, 3 ... #> 5   5 POLYGON ((4 0, 5 0, 5 1, 4 ... #> 6   6 POLYGON ((0 1, 1 1, 1 2, 0 ... #> 7   7 POLYGON ((1 1, 2 1, 2 2, 1 ... #> 8   8 POLYGON ((2 1, 3 1, 3 2, 2 ... #> 9   9 POLYGON ((3 1, 4 1, 4 2, 3 ... #> 10 10 POLYGON ((4 1, 5 1, 5 2, 4 ...   # Make a 5x10 grid covering a raster. r <- terra::rast(matrix(1:25, nrow = 5, ncol = 5), crs = "EPSG:4326") make_grid(r, cellsize = 1, n = c(10, 5)) #> Simple feature collection with 50 features and 1 field #> Geometry type: POLYGON #> Dimension:     XY #> Bounding box:  xmin: 0 ymin: 0 xmax: 10 ymax: 5 #> Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84 #> First 10 features: #>    id                       geometry #> 1   1 POLYGON ((0 0, 1 0, 1 1, 0 ... #> 2   2 POLYGON ((1 0, 2 0, 2 1, 1 ... #> 3   3 POLYGON ((2 0, 3 0, 3 1, 2 ... #> 4   4 POLYGON ((3 0, 4 0, 4 1, 3 ... #> 5   5 POLYGON ((4 0, 5 0, 5 1, 4 ... #> 6   6 POLYGON ((5 0, 6 0, 6 1, 5 ... #> 7   7 POLYGON ((6 0, 7 0, 7 1, 6 ... #> 8   8 POLYGON ((7 0, 8 0, 8 1, 7 ... #> 9   9 POLYGON ((8 0, 9 0, 9 1, 8 ... #> 10 10 POLYGON ((9 0, 10 0, 10 1, ...   # Make a grid using mininum and maximum coordinates and the number of grid # cells. make_grid_min_max_cells(   xy_min = c(-180, -90),   xy_max = c(180, 90),   n = c(14, 7),   crs = 4326 ) #> Simple feature collection with 98 features and 1 field #> Geometry type: POLYGON #> Dimension:     XY #> Bounding box:  xmin: -180 ymin: -90 xmax: 180 ymax: 90 #> Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84 #> First 10 features: #>    id                       geometry #> 1   1 POLYGON ((-180 -90, -154.28... #> 2   2 POLYGON ((-154.2857 -90, -1... #> 3   3 POLYGON ((-128.5714 -90, -1... #> 4   4 POLYGON ((-102.8571 -90, -7... #> 5   5 POLYGON ((-77.14286 -90, -5... #> 6   6 POLYGON ((-51.42857 -90, -2... #> 7   7 POLYGON ((-25.71429 -90, 0 ... #> 8   8 POLYGON ((0 -90, 25.71429 -... #> 9   9 POLYGON ((25.71429 -90, 51.... #> 10 10 POLYGON ((51.42857 -90, 77....   # Make a rectangular grid using square cells of 25 kilometers. make_grid_origin_dist(   xy_origin = c(-74, 4),   n = c(14, 7),   cell_size = 25000,   crs = 3116 ) #> Simple feature collection with 98 features and 1 field #> Geometry type: POLYGON #> Dimension:     XY #> Bounding box:  xmin: -74 ymin: 4 xmax: 349926 ymax: 175004 #> Projected CRS: MAGNA-SIRGAS / Colombia Bogota zone #> First 10 features: #>    id                       geometry #> 1   1 POLYGON ((-74 4, 24926 4, 2... #> 2   2 POLYGON ((24926 4, 49926 4,... #> 3   3 POLYGON ((49926 4, 74926 4,... #> 4   4 POLYGON ((74926 4, 99926 4,... #> 5   5 POLYGON ((99926 4, 124926 4... #> 6   6 POLYGON ((124926 4, 149926 ... #> 7   7 POLYGON ((149926 4, 174926 ... #> 8   8 POLYGON ((174926 4, 199926 ... #> 9   9 POLYGON ((199926 4, 224926 ... #> 10 10 POLYGON ((224926 4, 249926 ...   # Make a rectangular grid using square cells of 25 kilometers. make_grid_origin_res(   xy_origin = c(-74.2, 4.4),   xy_min = c(-78, -3),   xy_max = c(-70, 5),   cell_size = 0.5 ) #> Simple feature collection with 225 features and 1 field #> Geometry type: POLYGON #> Dimension:     XY #> Bounding box:  xmin: -77.7 ymin: -2.6 xmax: -70.2 ymax: 4.9 #> Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84 #> First 10 features: #>                                 x id #> 1  POLYGON ((-77.7 -2.6, -77.2...  1 #> 2  POLYGON ((-77.2 -2.6, -76.7...  2 #> 3  POLYGON ((-76.7 -2.6, -76.2...  3 #> 4  POLYGON ((-76.2 -2.6, -75.7...  4 #> 5  POLYGON ((-75.7 -2.6, -75.2...  5 #> 6  POLYGON ((-75.2 -2.6, -74.7...  6 #> 7  POLYGON ((-74.7 -2.6, -74.2...  7 #> 8  POLYGON ((-74.2 -2.6, -73.7...  8 #> 9  POLYGON ((-73.7 -2.6, -73.2...  9 #> 10 POLYGON ((-73.2 -2.6, -72.7... 10
